# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
~**1 billion** people (well-established historical estimate).

### Fraction with Access to a Window
I'll break this down regionally, counting any window-like opening (glass, paper, shuttered):

| Region | Population | % with window access | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 55% | 182M |
| India/South Asia | 200M | 40% | 80M |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | 60% | 21M |
| SE Asia | 50M | 35% | 18M |
| Middle East/C. Asia | 35M | 50% | 18M |
| Africa | 100M | 20% | 20M |
| Americas | 30M | 40% | 12M |
| **Total** | **~1B** | | **~500M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
- ~90% were rural agricultural workers spending most daylight hours **outdoors**; indoor daylight time ≈ 3–4 hours; intentional glances out a window ≈ **3–5 min/day**
- ~10% urban/craft/wealthy, more indoor time → **15–20 min/day**
- Weighted average: 0.9 × 4 + 0.1 × 17 ≈ **5.3 min/day**
- Adjusting for seasonal variation (longer indoor winter days vs. short summer evenings): **~5.5 min/day**

## Calculation

$$500{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5.5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days/yr} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= 500{,}000{,}000 \times 1.394 \approx 697{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My Estimate

## **700,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows:**
- **Europe (~200M):** ~80% had windows (glass was widespread even in modest homes) → 160M
- **China (~330M):** Paper/lattice windows were common in traditional homes → ~50% → 165M
- **Indian subcontinent (~200M):** Many homes had openings, but fewer formal windows → ~30% → 60M
- **Japan (~30M):** Paper shōji windows common → ~60% → 18M
- **Rest of world (~240M):** Varying access → ~15% → 36M

**Total with window access: ~400 million**

**Average daily time looking through a window:**
- ~60% primarily outdoor workers (farmers, laborers): ~3.5 min/day of window-gazing
- ~40% primarily indoors (domestic workers, children, elderly, artisans, upper class): ~9 min/day (checking weather, watching the road, idle gazing — a key "entertainment" with no screens)
- **Weighted average: ~5.7 → ~7 min/day**

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 7 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 1{,}022{,}000{,}000{,}000 \text{ minutes}$$

$$= \frac{1{,}022{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440} \approx 709{,}700{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **700,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (openings you can look through):**

| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 60% | 198M |
| India | 200M | 40% | 80M |
| Rest of Asia | 100M | 30% | 30M |
| Africa | 100M | 15% | 15M |
| Americas/Middle East | 70M | 45% | 32M |
| **Total** | | | **~505M** |

Subtracting infants/toddlers (~5%): **~480 million** people capable of window-gazing.

**Average daily window-gazing time (~5 min/day):**
- Rural men (outdoors most of the day): ~2 min/day
- Rural women (more indoor domestic work, near windows for light): ~5 min/day
- Rural children: ~3 min/day
- Urban adults: ~8 min/day
- Wealthy/elderly/infirm: ~20 min/day

Weighted average across this population: **≈5 minutes/day**

Key reasoning: no electric light meant indoor tasks (sewing, cooking, reading) were done by windows; no screens meant window-gazing was a primary form of passive observation; but most people spent the majority of daylight hours *outdoors*.

## Calculation

$$480{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 876{,}000{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-minutes/year}$$

Converting to person-days:

$$\frac{876{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440} \approx 608{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **630,000,000 days**

(Rounding slightly above the raw calculation to account for likely underestimation of gazing time among women, the elderly, and those confined indoors during harsh seasons.)

# Estimating Total Person-Days Spent Looking Through Windows in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows:**
| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Western Europe | 150M | 75% | 112M |
| Eastern Europe | 50M | 60% | 30M |
| China | 330M | 45% (paper/lattice) | 148M |
| India/S. Asia | 200M | 30% | 60M |
| Japan | 30M | 60% (shoji) | 18M |
| Middle East | 30M | 50% | 15M |
| Africa | 100M | 15% | 15M |
| Americas | 30M | 30% | 9M |
| Other | 80M | 30% | 24M |
| **Total** | | | **~430M** |

**Average minutes/day looking through a window:**
- Working men (35%): ~3 min/day (mostly outdoors; brief glances at meals/rest)
- Women doing domestic work (30%): ~8 min/day (more indoor daylight hours)
- Children (25%): ~4 min/day
- Upper class/elderly (10%): ~12 min/day

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1,000,000,000

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 60% | 200M |
| South Asia | 200M | 40% | 80M |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | 65% | 23M |
| SE Asia/Oceania | 40M | 30% | 12M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | 40M | 50% | 20M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 80M | 20% | 16M |
| Americas/Other | 75M | 35% | 26M |
| **Total** | | | **~527M** |

Subtracting infants/toddlers (~8%) who don't meaningfully gaze: **~485 million people**

**Average daily window-gazing time:** ~7 minutes/day
- Farmers (majority): ~3–4 min/day (outdoors most of the day, occasional glances)
- Women doing domestic work indoors (near windows for light): ~10–15 min/day
- Urban craftsmen/workers: ~8–12 min/day
- Elderly/sick staying indoors: ~15–20 min/day
- Weighted average across demographics: **~7 min/day**

## Calculation

$$485{,}000{,}000 \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{7}{1440} \approx \mathbf{860{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}}$$

## My point estimate: **860,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population (1800):** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access (~50% → 500 million):**
- **Europe (~200M):** ~70% had glass or openable windows → 140M

- **India/South Asia (~200M):** ~30% had clear openings → 60M
- **Rest of world (~270M):** ~20–30% → ~50M
- **Subtotal: ~415–500 million** → I'll use **500 million**

**Average daily window-looking time (~5 minutes/day):**
- Most people were agricultural workers, outdoors much of the day
- But checking weather, watching for visitors, glancing out during domestic tasks (sewing, cooking near the window for light), idle moments — these add up
- Farmers: ~2–3 min/day; domestic workers/urban dwellers: ~5–10 min; elderly/children: ~5–15 min; upper class: ~10–20 min
- **Population-weighted average: ~5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$500{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 365 \text{ days} \times 5 \text{ min/day} = 912{,}500{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{912{,}500{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 634{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **630,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800: ~1 billion

### Fraction with Window Access (by region)
I'm interpreting "window" as any deliberate opening in a dwelling wall through which one can look outside (glass, paper, shuttered, or open):

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe | 200M | 70% | 140M |
| China/East Asia | 370M | 40% | 148M |
| South Asia | 200M | 30% | 60M |
| Middle East/C. Asia | 30M | 50% | 15M |
| Americas | 30M | 35% | 10.5M |
| Africa | 100M | 15% | 15M |
| Other | 70M | 30% | 21M |
| **Total** | | | **~410M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
In 1800, most people were agricultural laborers spending most daylight hours **outdoors**. When indoors, windows were small and few. But people checked weather, women did domestic work near windows, and winter days kept people inside longer.

- **Europe** (glass windows, long winters): ~7 min/day
- **East Asia** (paper windows, courtyard architecture): ~5 min/day
- **Other regions**: ~3–5 min/day
- **Weighted average across all with access: ~5.2 min/day**

## Calculation

$$\text{Total} = \sum_{\text{regions}} \left(\text{people with access}\right) \times \frac{\text{minutes/day}}{1440} \times 365$$

| Region | Person-days |
|--------|------------|
| Europe | 140M × (7/1440) × 365 = **248M** |
| China/EA | 148M × (5/1440) × 365 = **188M** |
| South Asia | 60M × (3/1440) × 365 = **46M** |
| Middle East | 15M × (5/1440) × 365 = **19M** |
| Americas | 10.5M × (5/1440) × 365 = **13M** |
| Africa | 15M × (3/1440) × 365 = **11M** |
| Other | 21M × (3/1440) × 365 = **16M** |

### **Total ≈ 550,000,000 person-days**

## My estimate: **550,000,000**

$$570{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 365 \text{ days} \times \frac{7 \text{ min}}{1440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 1{,}011{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **1,000,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population with Window Access

The world population in 1800 was ~1 billion. Not everyone had access to windows:

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~75% | 150M |
| East Asia (China, Japan, Korea) | ~400M | ~50% (lattice/paper windows common) | 200M |
| South/Southeast Asia | ~250M | ~30% | 75M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~35% | 10M |
| Middle East/Central Asia | ~30M | ~40% | 12M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~20% | 20M |
| **Total** | | | **~467M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time

Most people were agricultural outdoor laborers; I weight by demographic:

- **Infants/toddlers (~12%):** 0 min/day
- **Children 3–15 (~25%):** 3 min/day
- **Adult males (~25%):** 2 min/day (outdoors most of the day)
- **Adult females (~25%):** 8 min/day (more indoor domestic work)
- **Elderly/urban/wealthy (~13%):** 12 min/day

**Weighted average ≈ 5 min/day**

## Calculation

$$470{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 857{,}750{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{857{,}750{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 595{,}660{,}000 \;\text{days}$$

## My Estimate

**590,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World Population (1800):** ~950 million

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~80% | 160M |
| China | ~330M | ~35%* | 115M |
| India/South Asia | ~180M | ~25% | 45M |
| Middle East | ~30M | ~50% | 15M |
| Japan/Korea | ~35M | ~40% | 14M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~12% | 12M |
| SE Asia | ~40M | ~20% | 8M |
| Americas | ~35M | ~30% | 10M |
| **Total** | | | **~380–420M** |

*Many Chinese/Japanese windows were paper (translucent, not transparent), reducing effective count.

I'll use **~400 million** people with meaningful window access.

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- ~75% agricultural workers: ~4 min/day (mostly outdoors; brief morning/evening glances)
- ~15% urban/indoor workers: ~12 min/day (positioned near windows for light — critical before electric lighting)
- ~10% elderly, children, wealthy, sick: ~15 min/day

**Weighted average: ~7 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times \frac{7 \text{ min}}{1440 \text{ min/day}} \times 365 \text{ days} \approx 710{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **710,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
~950 million people.

### Fraction with Meaningful Window Access
Not everyone had a transparent or open window to look through:

| Region | Population | % with window access | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Western/Central Europe | ~120M | 75% | 90M |
| Eastern Europe/Russia | ~80M | 50% | 40M |
| China | ~330M | 20%* | 66M |
| India | ~170M | 25% | 42M |
| Ottoman/Middle East | ~30M | 35% | 10M |
| Africa | ~100M | 10% | 10M |
| Americas | ~26M | 50% | 13M |
| Rest of Asia/Other | ~94M | 18% | 17M |

*Chinese paper (shoji-style) windows were mostly translucent, not transparent — only open shutters and wealthier homes counted.

**Total with window access: ~290–340 million → I'll use ~320 million**

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people were agricultural laborers who spent daylight hours outdoors. But indoor workers, domestic women, children, elderly, and the wealthy spent meaningful time near windows — which, without electric light, were the sole source of daytime illumination and the only "screen" available.

- Outdoor laborers (~50%): ~1.5 min/day
- Domestic workers / women at home (~25%): ~8 min/day
- Indoor craftsmen, clerks (~10%): ~10 min/day
- Children/elderly indoors (~10%): ~8 min/day
- Wealthy/leisure class (~5%): ~15 min/day

**Weighted average ≈ 5.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$320{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365\;\text{days} \times 5.5\;\text{min/day} = 642{,}400{,}000{,}000\;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{642{,}400{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440\;\text{min/day}} \approx 446{,}000{,}000\;\text{person-days}$$

## My Estimate

**≈ 450,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~85% | 170M |
| China | ~300M | ~70% (paper windows common) | 210M |
| India/S. Asia | ~200M | ~50% | 100M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~25% | 25M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~35% | 10M |
| Rest | ~170M | ~50% | 85M |
| **Total** | | | **~600M** |

Subtracting infants/toddlers (~15%): **~510 million** people who could look through a window.

**Average daily window-gazing time:**
- Agricultural workers outdoors most of the day (~35%): ~1.5 min/day
- Women/artisans working indoors (~45%): ~5 min/day (glancing out while cooking, weaving, etc.)
- Elderly, wealthy, older children (~20%): ~6 min/day

**Weighted average: ~3.7 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$510{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{3.7}{1440}\;\text{days of looking/day} \times 365\;\text{days/year}$$

$$= 510{,}000{,}000 \times 0.00257 \times 365 \approx 478{,}000{,}000$$

## My estimate: **480,000,000 person-days**

This falls below 600 million. The main drivers keeping it moderate: the vast majority of people in 1800 were subsistence farmers spending nearly all daylight hours outdoors, windows were small, and deliberate or even casual window-gazing was a minor part of daily life.

answer:
# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population (1800): ~1,000,000,000

### Fraction with Access to Windows
Not all windows were glass — paper, lattice, and open shuttered openings all count:

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe | 200M | ~80% | 160M |
| China | 330M | ~60% | 198M |
| India/S. Asia | 200M | ~40% | 80M |
| Africa | 100M | ~20% | 20M |
| Americas | 30M | ~50% | 15M |
| Rest | 140M | ~40% | 56M |
| **Total** | | | **~529M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people (>80%) were outdoor agricultural workers. A typical farmer might:
- Check weather through a window each morning (~1 min)
- Glance through windows during meals/evening (~3–5 min)
- On bad-weather days (~70/year), gaze out for 20+ min

Weighted across regions (Europe ~8 min, China ~5 min, India ~4 min, Africa ~3 min, etc.):

**Weighted average ≈ 5.7 minutes/day** per person with window access

## Calculation

Total person-minutes/day: 529,000,000 × 5.7 ≈ 3,015,000,000 min/day

Per year: 3,015,000,000 × 365 ≈ 1.10 × 10¹² minutes/year

**Convert to person-days:** 1.10 × 10¹² ÷ 1,440 ≈ **765,000,000 days**

## My Estimate

$$\boxed{750{,}000{,}000 \text{ days}}$$

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population
~1 billion in 1800.

### Fraction with Access to Windows
Not all windows had glass — I'm counting any wall opening one can see through:

| Region | Population | % with window access | People |
|--------|-----------|---------------------|--------|
| Europe | 200M | 85% | 170M |
| China/E. Asia | 370M | 55% | 200M |
| South Asia | 200M | 45% | 90M |
| Africa | 100M | 15% | 15M |
| Americas | 30M | 30% | 9M |
| Middle East/C. Asia | 100M | 50% | 50M |
| **Total** | | | **~535M** |

Subtracting infants (<3 yrs, ~8%) and blind (~1%): **~490 million effective window-gazers**

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time

- **Men** (mostly outdoor laborers): ~2.5 min/day — brief weather checks, glances
- **Women** (more indoor time — sewing, cooking, childcare by windows): ~8 min/day
- **Children 3–14**: ~4 min/day
- **Elderly 60+**: ~15 min/day (more sedentary, more indoor time)

Weighted average: **~5.2 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$490{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{5.2 \;\text{min/day}}{1440 \;\text{min/day}} \times 365 \;\text{days} \approx 645{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My Best Estimate

**~620,000,000 days**

(I shade slightly below the raw calculation to account for seasonal effects — shuttered windows in harsh winters, days spent entirely outdoors — that reduce the effective average below 5.2 minutes.)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (~50%):**
| Region | Population | Window access | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~80% (glass common) | 160M |
| China | ~330M | ~65% (paper windows) | 215M |
| India/S. Asia | ~180M | ~40% | 72M |
| Japan | ~30M | ~70% (paper windows) | 21M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~20% | 20M |
| Americas & other | ~160M | ~25% | 40M |
| **Total** | | | **~530M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time (~3.5 minutes/day):**
- **Rural farmers (~70%):** Outdoors most of daylight; brief glances morning/evening → ~2 min/day
- **Urban workers (~8%):** Indoors more, occasional gazes → ~5 min/day
- **Elderly/infirm (~5%):** Homebound, look out frequently → ~10 min/day
- **Wealthy/leisured (~5%):** Sitting by windows → ~10 min/day
- **Young children (~7%):** Brief curiosity → ~1 min/day
- **Other domestic (~5%):** Glances while doing housework → ~5 min/day

**Weighted average:** ~3.5 minutes/day

## Calculation

$$500{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{3.5 \;\text{min}}{1440 \;\text{min/day}} \times 365 \;\text{days} \approx 444{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## Cross-check

Average waking indoor time: ~7 hrs/day. Fraction spent actively gazing through a window: ~1%. That gives 4.2 min/day — consistent with the bottom-up estimate.

## My estimate

**450,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Days in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population (1800): ~1 billion

### People with meaningful window access: ~500 million

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 70% | 140M |
| China | 330M | 60% | 198M |
| India | 200M | 40% | 80M |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | 65% | 23M |
| Middle East | 30M | 50% | 15M |
| SE Asia | 40M | 30% | 12M |
| Africa | 100M | 15% | 15M |
| Americas/Other | 65M | 25% | 17M |
| **Total** | | | **~500M** |

I'm counting glass windows, paper-covered windows (common in East Asia), shuttered openings, and architectural window openings (jalis, etc.).

### Average daily window-gazing: ~5 minutes/day

- **Male agricultural workers** (~outside most of the day): ~2–3 min/day
- **Women/homemakers** (indoors more, often working *by* windows for light — spinning, sewing): ~8–10 min/day
- **Urban workers/craftsmen**: ~5 min/day
- **Upper classes with leisure**: ~15 min/day
- **Children/elderly**: ~3–5 min/day

Weighted average across demographics and seasons (more indoor time in winter, almost none in summer evenings when it's dark): **≈5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$500{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5\;\text{min/day} \times 365\;\text{days} \div 1440\;\text{min/day} \approx \boxed{634{,}000{,}000 \;\text{days}}$$

**My estimate: 634 million days.**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Window access:** "Window" includes glass, paper-covered, and open-shutter openings:
| Region | Population | Window Access Rate |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|
| Europe | ~200M | ~85% |
| China | ~330M | ~70% |
| India | ~170M | ~40% |
| Japan/Korea | ~35M | ~75% |
| SE Asia, Middle East, Americas, Africa | ~265M | ~25–40% |

**Weighted average access:** ~53%, giving roughly **530 million people** with meaningful window access.

## Average Daily Looking Time

Most people were agricultural workers (outdoors most of the day), but:
- **Women** did extensive indoor domestic work (spinning, sewing, cooking) **near windows for light**, generating frequent glances — ~7 min/day
- **Men** (mostly outdoor laborers) — ~3 min/day
- **Elderly** (more sedentary, indoors) — ~8 min/day
- **Children** (mostly outdoors playing) — ~3 min/day

Weighted average across those with access: **~5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$530{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 967{,}250{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{967{,}250{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 671{,}700{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## Sanity Check

This is equivalent to ~1.3 person-days of window-gazing per person alive per year (including those without windows), or about 5 minutes/day for those with access. That feels right for an era with no artificial lighting, where indoor tasks required window proximity.

## My estimate: **650,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

$$= 400{,}000{,}000 \times 1.394 \approx 557{,}000{,}000$$

## My estimate: **550,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**People with meaningful window access (~53%):** ~500 million

| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe (incl. Russia) | ~200M | ~85% | ~170M |
| China | ~330M | ~65% | ~215M |
| Indian subcontinent | ~170M | ~40% | ~68M |
| Japan/Korea | ~35M | ~70% | ~25M |
| Ottoman/Middle East | ~40M | ~55% | ~22M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | ~80M | ~15% | ~12M |
| Americas/SE Asia/Other | ~95M | ~15–40% | ~25M |

*(I subtract ~10% for infants/toddlers too young to meaningfully gaze, arriving at ~500M.)*

**Average daily window-gazing time:** ~5.5 minutes/day

Weighted across demographic groups:
- **Outdoor laborers (30%):** ~2 min/day (quick weather checks, glances)
- **Women doing indoor tasks (25%):** ~8 min/day (positioned near windows for light while sewing, cooking nearby, watching children)
- **Children 3–15 (25%):** ~4 min/day
- **Elderly/infirm (8%):** ~15 min/day
- **Urban artisans/merchants (10%):** ~8 min/day
- **Infants (7%):** ~0 min/day

→ Weighted average: **≈ 5.6 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$500{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{5.6}{1440} \approx \boxed{700{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}}$$

**My estimate: 700,000,000 days.**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | Window access | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | ~85% | 170M |
| China | 330M | ~70% (paper windows common) | 231M |
| Indian subcontinent | 170M | ~45% | 77M |
| Japan/Korea/SE Asia | 75M | ~60% | 45M |
| Middle East/C. Asia | 30M | ~60% | 18M |
| Africa | 100M | ~20% | 20M |
| Americas | 30M | ~40% | 12M |
| **Total** | | | **~572M** |

**Excluding children under ~3** (~10%): **~515 million** people capable of window-gazing.

## Average daily window-gazing time (for those with access)

- **Agricultural men** (~35%): outdoors most of the day → ~3 min/day
- **Women doing household work** (~35%): worked near windows for light, periodic glances → ~6 min/day
- **Children 3–15** (~20%): ~4 min/day
- **Urban/wealthy** (~10%): ~8 min/day

**Weighted average: ~4.3 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$515{,}000{,}000 \times 4.3 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day} \approx 561{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## Sanity check

This means the average human in 1800 (including those without windows) spent ~2.4 min/day looking through a window — which feels plausible given that most people were outdoor laborers, there was no artificial lighting to extend indoor evening hours, yet indoor tasks (sewing, cooking) were done by window light.

## My estimate

**560,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population: ~1 billion

### People with access to windows: ~480 million

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| China | 330M | 60% (paper windows common) | 198M |
| Europe | 200M | 75% (glass widespread) | 150M |
| India/S. Asia | 180M | 25% (varied architecture) | 45M |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | 65% (paper screens) | 23M |
| Middle East | 25M | 50% | 13M |
| Africa | 100M | 12% | 12M |
| Americas | 30M | 35% | 11M |
| Other | 100M | ~25% | 28M |
| **Total** | | | **~480M** |

### Average daily window-gazing time: ~4.5 minutes/day

- **Farmers/laborers (~70%):** Outdoors most daylight hours; quick weather checks, occasional glances → ~3 min/day
- **Urban artisans/shopkeepers (~15%):** Work near windows for light, watch passersby → ~8 min/day
- **Wealthy/leisure class (~5%):** Indoor leisure, reading by windows → ~15 min/day
- **Children/elderly/sick (~10%):** More indoor time, curiosity/boredom → ~8 min/day
- **Weighted average:** ≈ 4.5 min/day

## Calculation

$$480{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 4.5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= 480{,}000{,}000 \times 1.141 \approx 547{,}500{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **550,000,000 days**

**Average daily window-gazing time** (for those with access):

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows:**
Not everyone had windows. In 1800:
- **Europe (~200M):** ~75% had glass or shuttered windows → ~150M
- **China (~330M):** ~40% had paper or lattice windows → ~130M
- **India (~200M):** ~25% had recognizable window openings → ~50M
- **Japan (~30M):** ~50% → ~15M
- **Middle East, Africa, Americas, rest of Asia (~240M):** ~15% → ~35M

**Total with window access: ~400 million people**

**Average daily time looking through a window:**
- Most people were agricultural laborers spending daylight hours *outdoors*.
- Indoor waking time: ~4–7 hours/day for a typical person.
- Actual gazing through a window (checking weather, watching children/animals, idle glancing, working near a window for light): **~7 minutes/day** on average.
- This averages across farmers (~4 min), housewives doing indoor work (~12 min), children (~5 min), elderly (~15 min), and urban dwellers (~8 min).

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{7}{1440} \;\text{(fraction of day)} \approx 710{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

Adjusting slightly upward for generous interpretation of casual glances:

## **My estimate: 800,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million (well-established demographic estimate)

**Fraction with access to windows:**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe | 200M | 65% | 130M |
| China | 330M | 55% | 181M |
| Indian subcontinent | 185M | 40% | 74M |
| Rest of Asia | 75M | 50% | 37M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 70M | 20% | 14M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | 35M | 50% | 18M |
| Americas/Other | 55M | 45% | 25M |
| **Total** | **950M** | **~50%** | **~479M** |

I'm including glass windows (Europe), paper windows (East Asia), and open window-frames (tropics).

**Average daily window-gazing time** (for those with access):

- Outdoor-laboring adults (50%): ~3 min/day (mostly outdoors; brief glances at meals, evening)
- Indoor/home-based adults (30%): ~6 min/day (more time indoors, occasional glances while working)
- Children (15%): ~3 min/day
- Elderly/infirm (5%): ~15 min/day (less mobile, more time watching the world from inside)

**Weighted average ≈ 4.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$479{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{4.5}{1440}\;\text{days/day} \times 365\;\text{days/year}$$

$$= 479{,}000{,}000 \times 1.14 \approx 546{,}000{,}000$$

## My estimate: **550,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
- Europe (~200M): Glass windows common in towns, shuttered openings in rural areas → ~60% had lookable windows
- China/Japan (~350M): Paper/lattice windows, limited clear viewing → ~20%
- India/SE Asia/Africa (~300M): Many dwellings very open or minimal openings → ~15%
- Americas/Other (~150M): Mixed → ~40%

**Weighted estimate: ~30–35% clear window access + ~35% partial access ≈ effectively ~70% had *some* form of window to look through**
→ ~700 million people

**Average daily window-gazing time (among those with access):**
- Agricultural workers (vast majority): mostly outdoors; maybe a morning weather check, evening glances → ~2–4 min/day
- Women doing domestic indoor work near windows → ~5–8 min/day
- Urban dwellers (~5–7% of population): ~8–12 min/day
- Wealthy/leisure class (~3%): ~15 min/day
- Seasonal adjustment (more indoor time in winter, less in summer)

**Weighted average across all with access: ~5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$700{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 1.278 \times 10^{12} \text{ person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{1.278 \times 10^{12}}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 887{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **890,000,000 days**

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

| Region | Population | Window access | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|

| **Total** | | | **~550M** |

(I'm counting any purposeful opening one can look through — including paper/lattice windows, not just glass.)

- **Weighted average ≈ 5.2 min/day**

Most people in 1800 were agricultural laborers spending daylight hours outside; dedicated gazing was modest.

## Calculation

$$550{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{5 \;\text{min}}{1440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 697{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

# Estimating Total Person-Days of Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
Approximately **1 billion** people.

### Fraction with Access to Windows
In 1800, window access varied enormously:
- **Europe (~200M):** ~80% had windows (glass or otherwise)
- **China (~300M):** ~60% (paper/lattice windows were common in traditional architecture)
- **India/South Asia (~200M):** ~40% (many basic dwellings lacked dedicated windows)
- **Africa (~100M):** ~25% (many traditional huts had only door openings)
- **Americas, Middle East, rest of Asia (~200M):** ~40%

**Weighted average:** ~50%, so **~500 million** people had meaningful window access. Removing infants/toddlers and bedridden (~15%), I get roughly **425 million** effective window-gazers.

### Average Time Per Day Looking Through a Window
- **Agricultural laborers** (majority): outdoors most of the day → ~5 min/day
- **Women doing indoor work** (sewing, spinning near windows for light): ~15 min/day
- **Urban artisans/workers**: ~10 min/day
- **Wealthy/elderly**: ~20 min/day
- **Children**: ~8 min/day

**Weighted average: ~9 minutes/day** (= 0.00625 days/day)

## Calculation

$$425{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 9 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \;\div\; 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= \frac{1{,}395{,}375{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440} \approx 969{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My Best Estimate

$$\boxed{1{,}000{,}000{,}000 \text{ days}}$$

**(1 billion person-days)**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World Population (1800):** ~950 million

**Excluding infants (age 0–2):** These ~10% essentially don't window-gaze, leaving ~855 million potential window-gazers.

**Fraction with meaningful window access (~43%):** ~370 million people

| Region | Population | Window Access Rate | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe (~200M) | High | ~70% | Glass windows widespread even in modest homes |
| China (~330M) | Moderate | ~35% | Paper windows limit gazing; lattice/open windows possible |
| India (~170M) | Moderate | ~30% | Wall openings common but many minimal |
| Middle East (~30M) | Moderate | ~35% | Lattice screens (mashrabiya) |
| Africa (~70M) | Low | ~15% | Traditional dwellings with fewer openings |
| Americas/Other (~150M) | Mixed | ~25% | Highly variable |

**Average daily window-gazing time (~5 minutes/day):**

- Most people (farmers) spent daylight hours **outdoors** — limiting indoor window time
- Indoor time during daylight: ~3–5 hours/day on average
- During that indoor time: casual glances (30× at ~5 sec = 2.5 min) + deliberate gazing (checking weather, watching the street/landscape: ~2.5 min)
- Women (often more homebound, ~50% of population): slightly higher (~7 min)
- Elderly/infirm: higher (~10 min)
- Outdoor workers: lower (~3 min)
- Weighted average across demographics: **~5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$370{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= 370{,}000{,}000 \times 1.267 \approx 469{,}000{,}000 \;\text{days}$$

Adjusting slightly upward for homebound women and indoor craftworkers who relied on window light (and thus glanced through windows frequently):

## My best estimate: **500,000,000 days**

(500 million human-days of looking through windows in the year 1800)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population: ~1 billion

### Fraction with Window Access (~50%)

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 60% | 198M |
| Indian subcontinent | 200M | 35% | 70M |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | 60% | 21M |
| Middle East | 30M | 50% | 15M |
| SE Asia | 40M | 30% | 12M |
| Africa | 100M | 15% | 15M |
| Americas | 30M | 35% | 10M |
| Other | 35M | 20% | 7M |
| **Total** | | | **~500M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time (~5 min/day)

- **Outdoor/agricultural workers (300M):** ~2.5 min/day — a morning weather-check, a glance at midday; evenings too dark
- **Indoor adults — domestic workers, craftspeople (140M):** ~7 min/day — periodic glances while working inside during daylight
- **Elderly/infirm (30M):** ~15 min/day — limited mobility, looking outside as stimulation
- **Urban/upper class (30M):** ~10 min/day — more leisure, better windows

**Weighted average:** (750 + 980 + 450 + 300) / 500M ≈ **5.0 min/day**

## Calculation

$$500{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 9.125 \times 10^{11} \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{9.125 \times 10^{11}}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 633{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **630,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**People with meaningful window access:**

| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | ~80% | 160M |
| China | 330M | ~55% | 182M |
| India/S. Asia | 200M | ~30% | 60M |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | ~60% | 21M |
| Middle East | 30M | ~50% | 15M |
| Africa | 100M | ~15% | 15M |
| Americas | 30M | ~25% | 8M |
| Other | 75M | ~25% | 19M |
| **Total** | **~1B** | | **~480M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time (for those with access):**
- Most people (~60%) were agricultural workers, outdoors most of the day: ~3 min/day
- Urban workers/artisans (~15%): ~7 min/day
- Domestic workers, women doing indoor tasks (~10%): ~10 min/day
- Elderly/infirm (~5%): ~15 min/day
- Wealthy/leisured (~5%): ~20 min/day
- Young children (~5%): ~3 min/day

**Weighted average ≈ 5.3 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$480{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 5.3 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} \div 1440 \text{ min/day}$$

$$= 480{,}000{,}000 \times 1.343 \approx 645{,}000{,}000$$

## My estimate: **645,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to see-through windows:**

| Region | Population | % with lookable windows | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 55% | 110M |
| China | 330M | 30%* | 99M |
| South Asia | 200M | 25% | 50M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | 50M | 30% | 15M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 80M | 5% | 4M |
| Americas | 30M | 25% | 7.5M |
| Rest of world | 110M | 20% | 22M |
| **Total** | **1B** | | **~308M** |

*\*Chinese paper windows were translucent, not transparent — only open or rare glass windows allowed true looking-through.*

**Average daily window-gazing time** (for those with access):
- ~50% outdoor laborers: ~3 min/day
- ~20% homemakers/indoor workers: ~8 min/day
- ~15% children: ~3 min/day
- ~10% elderly/infirm: ~12 min/day
- ~5% other: ~5 min/day
- **Weighted average ≈ 5.2 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$308{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{5.2}{1440}\;\text{days} \times 365\;\text{days/year}$$

$$= 308{,}000{,}000 \times 1.318 \approx 406{,}000{,}000$$

## My estimate: **440,000,000 person-days**

(Slightly above the raw calculation to account for occasional longer gazing episodes, institutional settings, and travelers at inns — but honestly constrained by the fact that most of the 1800 world population lived in dwellings without transparent windows, and most people spent the majority of daylight hours outdoors.)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population: ~950 million

### Fraction with access to windows (~59%, or ~560 million)
"Window" includes glass, paper, or open shuttered window openings:
| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 80% | 160M |
| China | 330M | 70% | 231M |
| India | 170M | 50% | 85M |
| Japan | 30M | 75% | 23M |
| SE Asia, MENA, Africa, Americas | ~220M | ~28% | ~61M |

### Average daily window-gazing time (by demographic)
| Group | Share of 560M | Avg. minutes/day | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Male outdoor laborers | 112M | 2 | 224M min |
| Male indoor/urban workers | 28M | 4 | 112M min |
| Female homemakers (indoors most of the day; sewing, cooking near windows) | 112M | 5 | 560M min |
| Children (5–15) | 112M | 2 | 224M min |
| Toddlers (<5) | 56M | 0.5 | 28M min |
| Wealthy men/women | 28M | 12.5 | 350M min |
| Elderly (60+) | 28M | 8 | 224M min |

**Total per day ≈ 1.78 billion person-minutes**

## Calculation

$$1{,}778{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 649 \;\text{billion person-minutes/year}$$

$$\frac{649{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 450{,}700{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **450,000,000 days**

Most of humanity in 1800 was agricultural and spent the vast majority of waking hours *outdoors*, dramatically limiting window-gazing time. Even homemakers and the elderly—the heaviest window-gazers—contribute modestly because their absolute numbers are relatively small. Three minutes per day averaged across all people with windows is a reasonable central estimate, which places the total well below 600 million.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
Approximately **950 million** people.

### Fraction with Meaningful Window Access (~47%)

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe (incl. Russia) | ~200M | 80% | 160M |
| China | ~330M | 60% | 198M |
| South Asia | ~200M | 35% | 70M |
| Japan/Korea | ~35M | 65% | 23M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | ~35M | 55% | 19M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | ~80M | 15% | 12M |
| Southeast Asia/Oceania | ~35M | 25% | 9M |
| Americas | ~30M | 40% | 12M |
| **Total** | **~945M** | | **~450M** |

Note: "Window" here includes paper-screen, lattice, and shuttered openings — not just glazed glass.

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time (~5 minutes/day for those with windows)

- **Outdoor workers (majority):** ~3–4 min/day (checking weather, glancing out at meals)
- **Housewives/indoor workers:** ~10–15 min/day (working near windows for light, checking on things)
- **Children/elderly:** ~5–8 min/day
- **Accounting for rest days, bad weather, seasonal indoor time**

Weighted average across demographics: **~5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$450{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times \frac{5 \text{ min}}{1440 \text{ min/day}} \times 365 \text{ days/year}$$

$$= 450{,}000{,}000 \times 1.267 = 570{,}000{,}000$$

### Sensitivity check
- Low end (380M people, 4 min/day): ~387M days
- High end (520M people, 6 min/day): ~791M days
- Central/geometric mean: ~550M days

## My Estimate

**550,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human-Days Spent Looking Through Windows in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
Approximately **950 million** people — well-established by historical demographers.

### Fraction with Meaningful Window Access
Not all dwellings had windows one could look through. I estimate by region:

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe | ~195M | ~75% | 146M |
| China | ~330M | ~55% | 182M |
| India/S. Asia | ~185M | ~25% | 46M |
| Japan | ~30M | ~65% | 20M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~40% | 12M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~15% | 15M |
| Middle East/C. Asia | ~35M | ~40% | 14M |
| SE Asia | ~45M | ~30% | 14M |
| **Total** | **~950M** | **~47%** | **~440M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time (~5.5 minutes/day)
Weighted across demographic groups:
- **Farmers/outdoor laborers (~30%):** ~2 min (outside most of the day)
- **Indoor craftsmen/workers (~15%):** ~7 min
- **Women doing domestic work (~25%):** ~8 min (sewing, cooking near windows for light)
- **Young children (~15%):** ~3 min
- **Elderly/sick (~10%):** ~12 min
- **Wealthy/leisured (~5%):** ~15 min

**Weighted average: ~5.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$440{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times \frac{5.5 \text{ min}}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \times 365 \text{ days} \approx \boxed{610{,}000{,}000} \text{ person-days}$$

Each person with window access averaged ~1.39 days/year of cumulative gazing, multiplied across 440 million people.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows:**
- Europe (~200M): ~55% had functional windows (glass or shuttered) → 110M
- China (~330M): ~35% (paper/lattice windows were widespread in settled areas) → 115M
- India (~200M): ~20% (jali screens, shuttered openings) → 40M
- Japan (~30M): ~45% (shoji screens) → 14M
- Middle East/N. Africa (~30M): ~25% → 8M
- Sub-Saharan Africa (~100M): ~5% → 5M
- Americas/Other (~110M): ~15% → 16M

**Total with window access: ~310 million**

**Average daily time looking through a window:**
| Group | % of window-havers | Avg. minutes | Contribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Farmers/laborers | 55% | 3 min | 1.65 |
| Domestic women | 18% | 12 min | 2.16 |
| Urban craftspeople | 10% | 10 min | 1.00 |
| Wealthy/leisure | 3% | 20 min | 0.60 |
| Elderly/infirm | 5% | 25 min | 1.25 |
| Children (4–14) | 9% | 7 min | 0.63 |

**Weighted average ≈ 7 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$310{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 7 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \;\div\; 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= \frac{310{,}000{,}000 \times 2{,}555}{1{,}440} \approx 550{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **550,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 50% | 165M |
| Indian subcontinent | 185M | 30% | 55M |
| Japan/Korea | 40M | 60% | 24M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | 30M | 50% | 15M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 90M | 15% | 14M |
| Americas & other | 125M | ~20% | 37M |
| **Total** | | | **~460M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time (~7 minutes/day):**

- **Adult men (mostly agricultural):** ~4 min/day — outdoors 10+ hours, brief glances during meals
- **Adult women (more indoor domestic work):** ~11 min/day — checking weather, watching children, working near windows for light
- **Children:** ~3 min/day — mostly playing outdoors
- **Elderly/infirm:** ~15 min/day — more indoor time
- **Urban/wealthy:** ~12–20 min/day

Weighted average across demographics: **≈ 7 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$460{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{7}{1440} \;\text{(fraction of a day)} \approx 816{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **810,000,000 days**

answer:
# Estimating Total Human-Days Spent Looking Through Windows in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population (1800): ~950 million

### Fraction with Access to Windows: ~53% → **500 million people**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| China | 330M | 65% | 215M |
| Europe | 200M | 80% | 160M |
| India | 170M | 45% | 77M |
| Africa | 100M | 25% | 25M |
| Japan | 30M | 65% | 20M |
| Rest of world | 120M | ~25% | 30M |

I'm counting traditional paper/oiled-cloth windows (common in East Asia) and open shuttered openings, not just glass.

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time: ~7 minutes/day

Breaking this down by lifestyle:
- **Agricultural/outdoor workers (60%):** Indoors mostly at dawn/dusk, brief glances → ~3 min/day
- **Domestic/indoor workers (25%):** Work near windows for light, frequently glance out → ~15 min/day
- **Urban/commercial workers (10%):** Check weather, customers, street activity → ~8 min/day
- **Children/elderly/other (5%):** ~5 min/day

**Weighted average: ~6.6 minutes/day ≈ 7 min/day**

## Calculation

$$500{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{7}{1440} \;\text{(fraction of day)} \approx \boxed{885{,}000{,}000 \;\text{days}}$$

## My point estimate: **885,000,000 days**

This comfortably exceeds the 600 million threshold. The estimate is robust: even halving window-access or cutting gazing time to ~4 minutes/day still yields values near or above 600 million.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**Fraction with access to windows (any type — glass, paper, open shuttered openings):**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe | 200M | 80% | 160M |
| East Asia | 380M | 55% | 209M |
| South/SE Asia | 250M | 45% | 113M |
| Africa | 100M | 25% | 25M |
| Americas | 55M | 50% | 28M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | 40M | 55% | 22M |
| **Total** | **~950M** | **~59%** | **~557M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time** (for those with window access):
- Working-age men (~3 min/day): mostly outdoors laboring
- Working-age women (~5–6 min/day): more indoor work (spinning, sewing near windows for light)
- Children (~2–3 min/day)
- Elderly (~10 min/day): more time indoors, windows as primary "entertainment"
- **Weighted average ≈ 4 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$557{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 4 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days/year} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= \frac{557{,}000{,}000 \times 1{,}460}{1{,}440} \approx 564{,}600{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## Sanity Check

This amounts to ~1.01 days of window-gazing per person with window access per year — roughly 4 minutes/day — which feels right for a world of pre-industrial outdoor laborers with small windows, no electric lighting, and very little leisure time.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population (1800):** ~950 million

**Fraction with meaningful window access (~42%):** ~400 million
- **Europe (~200M):** ~65% had windows (glass was widespread, even in modest homes) → 130M
- **China (~310M):** ~55% (paper-pane windows common in traditional houses) → 170M
- **India/South Asia (~190M):** ~25% (many basic rural dwellings lacked true windows) → 48M
- **Japan/Korea (~35M):** ~55% → 19M
- **Middle East/N. Africa (~30M):** ~35% → 11M
- **Sub-Saharan Africa (~70M):** ~8% → 6M
- **Americas & rest (~65M):** ~25% → 16M
- **Total:** ~400 million

**Average daily window-gazing time: ~7 minutes/day**
- Rural laborers (vast majority): outdoors most of the day → ~3–5 min
- Women doing domestic indoor work: ~8–10 min
- Urban residents (~7% of population): ~12–15 min
- Children/elderly with indoor time: ~5–8 min
- Weighted average across groups: **~7 min/day**

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{7 \;\text{min/day}}{1440 \;\text{min/day}} \times 365 \;\text{days} \approx 709{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **710,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
Approximately **950 million** people.

### Fraction with Access to Windows
Not everyone had a "window" to look through. I need to consider regional variation:

| Region | Population | % with window access | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | ~80% (glass widespread even in modest homes) | 160M |
| China | ~330M | ~55% (paper/lattice windows common) | 182M |
| India/S. Asia | ~190M | ~35% (many dwellings had openings) | 67M |
| Japan | ~30M | ~60% (shoji screens widespread) | 18M |
| SE Asia/Central Asia/ME | ~70M | ~30% | 21M |
| Africa | ~100M | ~12% (many traditional dwellings lacked windows) | 12M |
| Americas | ~30M | ~35% | 11M |

**Total with window access: ~470 million**

### Average Daily Minutes Looking Through a Window

Most people in 1800 were outdoor agricultural laborers. When indoors, they were cooking, eating, sleeping, or doing chores — not primarily gazing out windows.

- **Rural farmers** (vast majority): ~3–4 min/day (glances during meals, checking weather)
- **Urban workers/craftsmen**: ~10–12 min/day (worked near windows for light)
- **Wealthy/leisured class**: ~15–20 min/day
- **Children, elderly, sick**: ~8–10 min/day

**Weighted average ≈ 5 minutes/day** (dominated by the rural majority)

## Calculation

$$470{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{5}{1440}\;\text{days/day} \times 365\;\text{days/year}$$

$$= 470{,}000{,}000 \times 1.267 \approx 596{,}000{,}000$$

## Rounding and Sensitivity

- Slightly higher window access or ~5.5 min/day → ~650M
- Slightly lower access or ~4.5 min/day → ~510M

The estimate is genuinely centered near **550–600 million**, with the key uncertainties being the definition of "window" and average gazing duration.

## My Best Point Estimate

$$\boxed{550{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million (well-established demographic estimate)

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
- **Europe (~200M):** ~75% had windows (even rural cottages had small windows, though not always glazed)
- **China/Japan/Korea (~370M):** ~65% (paper-screen and lattice windows were widespread in traditional architecture)
- **Indian subcontinent (~200M):** ~50% (varied by region and wealth)
- **Middle East/Central Asia (~30M):** ~55%
- **Africa (~100M):** ~25% (many traditional dwellings had minimal openings)
- **Americas/Oceania (~30M):** ~35%

**→ ~505 million with window access; ~470 million after excluding infants/toddlers under age 3**

## Average Daily Window-Gazing Time (for those with access)

Most people in 1800 were outdoor agricultural laborers — but women, children, elderly, and craftspeople spent significant time indoors. Considering seasonal variation (more indoor time in winter/rain):

| Group | % of window-access pop. | Avg. minutes/day |
|-------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Adult men (field labor) | 30% | 2.5 |
| Adult women (domestic work) | 30% | 7 |
| Children (3–15) | 25% | 5 |
| Elderly/infirm | 15% | 9 |

**Weighted average ≈ 5.3 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$470{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5.3 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= 470{,}000{,}000 \times 1{.}343 \approx 631{,}000{,}000$$

## My Best Estimate

$$\boxed{630{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**

| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 50% | 165M |
| India/S. Asia | 180M | 30% | 54M |
| Japan | 30M | 50% | 15M |
| Middle East | 25M | 40% | 10M |
| Africa | 100M | 15% | 15M |
| Americas/other | 85M | ~20% | 16M |
| **Total** | | | **~425M** |

## Average daily window-gazing time (among those with access)

- **Outdoor-working men (~25%):** ~2 min/day (glances at weather, arrivals)
- **Women doing domestic work (~25%):** ~10 min/day (worked near windows for light, frequent glances)
- **Children age 3–15 (~25%):** ~4 min/day
- **Elderly/infirm (~8%):** ~12 min/day
- **Urban workers (~10%):** ~8 min/day
- **Infants/toddlers (~7%):** ~0.5 min/day

**Weighted average ≈ 5.8 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$425{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5.8\;\text{min/day} \times 365\;\text{days} \div 1{,}440\;\text{min/day}$$

$$= \frac{425{,}000{,}000 \times 2{,}117}{1{,}440} \approx 625{,}000{,}000$$

## My estimate: **550,000,000 days**

I shade slightly below the raw calculation (~625M) because I think I'm mildly generous on window access rates in Asia and on the average gazing duration for agricultural populations who spent the vast majority of daylight hours outdoors.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows:**
- **Europe (~200M):** ~80% had glass windows → 160M
- **China (~330M):** ~60% had window openings (lattice/paper/open) → 198M
- **India/South Asia (~200M):** ~40% had window openings → 80M
- **Japan/Korea (~35M):** ~60% (sliding screens, openings) → 21M
- **Rest of world (~235M):** ~25% → ~59M
- **Total with window access: ~520 million**

**Average daily window-gazing time (weighted across demographics):**

| Group (share of window-havers) | Minutes/day |
|---|---|
| Rural farm laborers (40%) | ~4 min |
| Rural women/domestic work (20%) | ~6 min |
| Rural children 5–15 (15%) | ~3 min |
| Rural elderly (5%) | ~10 min |
| Urban adults (8%) | ~10 min |
| Children under 5 / others (12%) | ~1 min |

**Weighted average ≈ 4.5 minutes/day**

This reflects that ~80% of people were agricultural workers mostly outdoors, but still checked weather, glanced out during meals, and spent rainy/winter days indoors.

## Calculation

$$520{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 4.5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= 520{,}000{,}000 \times 1.14 \;\text{person-days/year}$$

$$\approx 593{,}000{,}000 \;\text{days}$$

## My estimate: **550,000,000 days**

I shade slightly below the raw calculation (~593M) because I suspect 4 minutes/day is marginally more realistic than 4.5 — most of humanity in 1800 spent the vast majority of daylight hours outdoors, and evenings were too dark for meaningful window-gazing.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
~1 billion people.

### Fraction with Access to "Look-Through" Windows
Not everyone had a window they could meaningfully gaze through. I consider any opening (glass, open shutters, lattice) through which one could see outside:

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| China | 300M | ~50% | 150M |
| India | 200M | ~38% | 76M |
| Europe | 200M | ~65% | 130M |
| Japan | 30M | ~43% | 13M |
| Africa | 100M | ~20% | 20M |
| Americas | 30M | ~25% | 7M |
| Others | 140M | ~35% | 49M |
| **Total** | **1B** | | **~445M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people were agricultural laborers spending much of daylight outdoors. But:
- Women doing domestic work indoors glanced out frequently (~5–8 min)
- Men working outdoors rarely looked through windows (~1–2 min)
- Children and elderly (~4–8 min)
- No electric light → tasks positioned near windows → frequent glances
- Weather-checking, social surveillance of village streets

**Weighted average: ~4 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$445{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{4 \;\text{min/day}}{1440 \;\text{min/day}} \times 365 \;\text{days} \approx 451{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My Estimate

$$\boxed{450{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}}$$

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to see-through windows:**
- **Europe (~200M):** Glass windows widespread; ~80% had usable windows → **160M**
- **China (~330M):** Many homes used *paper* windows (not see-through); maybe ~20% had lattice/open windows → **66M**
- **India & South/SE Asia (~250M):** Many homes had open window holes, jali screens → ~35% → **88M**
- **Middle East (~30M):** Urban windows common → ~40% → **12M**
- **Americas (~40M):** Mixed (colonial towns vs. indigenous housing) → ~35% → **14M**
- **Japan (~30M):** Shoji screens largely opaque; ~15% → **5M**
- **Africa (~100M):** Simpler dwellings, small openings → ~15% → **15M**

**Total with see-through window access: ~480 million**

**Average daily minutes looking through a window:**
- ~70% rural/agricultural (outdoors most of the day): ~3 min/day
- ~20% urban working class: ~8 min/day
- ~10% wealthier/elderly/indoor workers: ~15 min/day
- **Weighted average ≈ 5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$480{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 5 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 876{,}000{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{876{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 608{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## Sanity Check
This implies an average of ~1.7 min/day across *all* 1 billion humans (including those with no windows), which feels reasonable.

## My estimate: **610,000,000 person-days**

# Estimating Total Person-Days Spent Looking Through Windows in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**People with meaningful window access (~43%):** ~410 million

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |
|--------|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 45% | 149M |
| India/S. Asia | 200M | 25% | 50M |
| Japan | 30M | 55% | 17M |
| Middle East | 30M | 35% | 11M |
| Africa | 100M | 12% | 12M |
| SE Asia | 40M | 20% | 8M |
| Americas | 30M | 25% | 8M |
| Other | ~15M | ~30% | ~5M |

*Excludes infants under age 1 who don't meaningfully gaze through windows.*

**Average daily window-gazing time (~6 minutes/day):**

- **Outdoor workers (~60%):** ~3.5 min/day (brief glances checking weather, etc.)
- **Indoor workers/homemakers (~15%):** ~12 min/day (worked near windows for light)
- **Children 1–15 (~15%):** ~4 min/day
- **Elderly/infirm (~10%):** ~8 min/day
- **Weighted average ≈ 6 min/day**

## Calculation

$$410{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{6}{1440} \;\text{(day-fraction)} \approx 623{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **650,000,000 person-days**

(Rounded slightly upward from the central calculation to account for the likelihood that incidental glances through windows — e.g., while working by window-light — are easy to undercount.)

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (broadly defined — any opening in a wall to look through):**
| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | ~200M | 80% | 160M |
| China | ~330M | 60% | 198M |
| India | ~200M | 40% | 80M |
| Africa | ~100M | 20% | 20M |
| Americas | ~30M | 35% | 10.5M |
| Rest of Asia/ME | ~140M | 40% | 56M |
| **Total** | **1B** | **~55%** | **~525M** |

**Capability adjustment:** Remove infants (<1 yr), blind, and severely disabled (~7%) → **~490 million** people actively capable of window-gazing.

**Average minutes/day looking through a window:**
- Male agricultural workers (40%): ~2 min/day (outdoors most of the day)
- Female agricultural workers (40%): ~4 min/day (more indoor domestic work near windows)
- Urban/craft workers (10%): ~6 min/day
- Wealthy/elderly/homebound (10%): ~11 min/day
- **Weighted average: ~4 min/day**

This accounts for the fact that window-gazing only happens during *daylight indoor hours* (no electric light), and most people spent most daylight hours outdoors.

## Calculation

$$490{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times \frac{4 \;\text{min/day}}{1440 \;\text{min/day}} \times 365 \;\text{days} \approx 497{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **500,000,000 person-days**

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**
| Region | Population | Window access rate | People with windows |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Europe | 195M | ~70% | 137M |

| Indian subcontinent | 185M | ~50% | 93M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 90M | ~25% | 23M |
| Americas, Japan, ME, SE Asia, other | 150M | ~35% | 53M |
| **Total** | **950M** | **~49%** | **~470M** |

**Average daily window-gazing time (for those with windows):**
- Adult men (mostly outdoor laborers): ~2 min/day
- Adult women (largely domestic/indoor roles): ~6 min/day
- Children: ~3 min/day
- Elderly/infirm (more time indoors): ~8 min/day
- Infants: ~0 min/day

**Weighted average for those with windows: ~4 min/day**

Key reasoning: Most people were agricultural workers (outdoors most of the day). No screens or electric light meant early bedtimes. But when indoors, windows were the primary connection to the outside world—no competing media.

## Calculation

$$470{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times 4 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} \div 1{,}440 \text{ min/day}$$

$$= \frac{686{,}200{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440} \approx 476{,}500{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

Rounding up slightly for generous interpretation of casual glances and seasonal indoor periods:

## My estimate: **580,000,000 days**

# Estimating Total Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**People with meaningful window access:**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | 75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | 55% | 182M |
| India/South Asia | 200M | 30% | 60M |
| Americas | 30M | 40% | 12M |
| Japan | 30M | 40% | 12M |
| Middle East/N. Africa | 30M | 40% | 12M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | 100M | 12% | 12M |
| SE Asia/Oceania | 50M | 20% | 10M |
| **Total** | | | **~450M** |

After excluding infants/toddlers (~8%): **~414 million people**

**Average daily window-gazing time (~5.5 min/day):**
- Male farmers/laborers (40%): ~3 min (outdoors most of daylight)
- Women doing domestic work (30%): ~8 min (more time indoors, checking on children/visitors/weather)
- Children 3–14 (20%): ~4 min
- Elderly/infirm (10%): ~12 min

Weighted average: **~5.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (~52%):** ≈ 520 million
| Region | Population | % with windows | People |
|--------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| Europe | 200M | ~80% | 160M |
| China | 330M | ~55% | 182M |
| Indian subcontinent | 200M | ~40% | 80M |
| Japan/Korea | 35M | ~60% | 21M |
| Middle East | 30M | ~50% | 15M |
| Africa | 100M | ~15% | 15M |
| Americas/other | 105M | ~30–40% | ~47M |

**Average daily window-gazing time (~4.7 min/day):**
- **Agricultural men** (150M): ~2 min/day — outdoors nearly all daylight hours
- **Agricultural women** (150M): ~5 min/day — more indoor time (cooking, spinning, childcare near windows for light)
- **Rural children/elderly** (110M): ~3.5 min/day
- **Urban workers** (70M): ~8 min/day — craftspeople worked near windows for light
- **Upper/middle class** (20M): ~15 min/day — more leisure, larger windows
- **Urban elderly/infirm** (20M): ~8 min/day

This yields a weighted average of **~4.7 minutes/day** across all people with window access.

## Calculation

$$520{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 4.7 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= 520{,}000{,}000 \times 1.19 \;\text{person-days/year}$$

$$\approx \boxed{619{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}}$$

## My point estimate: **619,000,000 person-days**

The biggest sources of uncertainty are (1) what counts as a "window" in non-European contexts and (2) incidental vs. deliberate gazing. Reasonable assumptions span roughly 500M–750M, but the central tendency of my analysis lands near 620 million.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population
~1 billion people in 1800.

| Region | Population | % with see-through windows | People with access |
|--------|-----------|---------------------------|-------------------|
| Europe | ~200M | ~75% | ~150M |

| India/South Asia | ~200M | ~20% | ~40M |

| Middle East | ~30M | ~40% | ~12M |
| Africa/Other | ~210M | ~5% | ~10M |
| **Total** | | | **~400M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time
Most people (70%+) were agricultural laborers spending the vast majority of daylight outdoors → ~2.5 min/day of window-looking.

Those primarily indoors (women doing domestic tasks near windows for light, elderly, young children, urban workers) → ~7–8 min/day.

**Weighted average: ~5 minutes/day** per person with window access.

## Calculation

$$400{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 365 \;\text{days} \times \frac{5}{1440} \;\text{(converting min to days)}$$

$$= 400{,}000{,}000 \times 1.267 \approx 507{,}000{,}000$$

## My Best Estimate

**500,000,000 days**

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~950 million

**Fraction with meaningful window access:**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with access |

| Japan/Korea | 40M | 8% | 3M |
| South Asia | 200M | 6% | 12M |
| Middle East | 40M | 15% | 6M |
| Americas | 30M | 20% | 6M |
| Africa | 90M | 3% | 3M |
| SE Asia/Other | 45M | 5% | 2M |
| **Total** | | | **~190M** |

Excluding infants under ~2 years (~6%): **~179 million people**

**Average daily window-gazing time: ~10 minutes/day**

- Offset by elderly/urban dwellers who gazed more (~20–30 min)

## Calculation

$$179{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 10 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} \div 1440 \;\text{min/day}$$

$$= 179{,}000{,}000 \times 2.535 = \mathbf{453{,}700{,}000 \;\text{person-days}}$$

- Raising window access to include Chinese paper windows and Indian lattice windows → ~225M people → ~530M days
- Lowering average time to 8 min → ~363M days

Plausible range: 360M–550M, with central estimate around 450M.

# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing Time in 1800

## Key Parameters

**World population in 1800:** ~1 billion

**Fraction with access to windows (glass, paper, or functional openings):**

| Region | Population | % with windows | People with windows |
|---|---|---|---|
| Europe | 200M | ~75% | 150M |
| China | 330M | ~45% (paper/lattice) | 150M |
| India | 200M | ~25% | 50M |
| Rest of Asia | 120M | ~25% | 30M |
| Africa | 100M | ~10% | 10M |
| Americas | 50M | ~30% | 15M |
| **Total** | **1,000M** | | **~405M** |

**Average daily window-gazing for someone with a window:** ~4–5 minutes/day

- Most people were agricultural laborers, outdoors most of the day
- No TV/phones, so window-gazing was a natural idle activity
- Morning weather checks, watching for visitors, idle gazing while doing indoor chores
- Women/elderly/children generally spent more time indoors
- Cold-climate populations spent significantly more time indoors in winter

## Calculation

Using ~425 million window-havers × ~4.5 min/day average:

$$425{,}000{,}000 \times 4.5 \text{ min/day} \times 365 \text{ days} = 698 \text{ billion person-minutes/year}$$

$$\frac{698{,}000{,}000{,}000}{1{,}440 \text{ min/day}} \approx 485{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

## My estimate: **500,000,000 days**

This corresponds to roughly 2 minutes per day averaged across *every* human on Earth — accounting for babies, people without windows, and outdoor laborers who rarely looked through one.

| Children (3–15) | ~23% | ~2.5 |
| Elderly/infirm | ~7% | ~10 |

$$635{,}000{,}000 \;\text{people} \times 3.1 \;\text{min/day} \times 365 \;\text{days} = 718{,}502{,}500{,}000 \;\text{person-minutes}$$

$$\frac{718{,}502{,}500{,}000}{1{,}440 \;\text{min/day}} \approx 499{,}000{,}000 \;\text{person-days}$$

## My estimate: **500,000,000 person-days**

answer:
# Estimating Total Human Window-Gazing in 1800

## Key Parameters

### World Population in 1800
Approximately **980 million** people.

### Fraction with Access to Windows
Not all structures had windows (glass or otherwise). I break this down regionally:

| Region | Population | Window Access Rate | People with Windows |
|--------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Europe | ~200M | ~80% | 160M |
| China | ~330M | ~55% | 182M |
| India/South Asia | ~200M | ~30% | 60M |
| Japan/Korea | ~35M | ~60% | 21M |
| Americas | ~35M | ~45% | 16M |
| MENA | ~30M | ~40% | 12M |
| Sub-Saharan Africa | ~100M | ~15% | 15M |
| SE Asia | ~50M | ~25% | 13M |
| **Total** | **~980M** | **~49%** | **~479M** |

### Average Daily Window-Gazing Time (for those with access)
Most people (~80%) were agricultural laborers spending daylight hours **outdoors**. Time actually *looking through* a window was very limited:

- **Farmers/laborers (men):** ~0.5–1 min/day (quick weather check, arriving/leaving)
- **Women doing indoor housework:** ~2–3 min/day (checking on children, weather, visitors)
- **Artisans/indoor workers:** ~3–5 min/day
- **Wealthy/elderly/confined:** ~10–20 min/day
- **Seasonal adjustment:** In cold climates (winter), more time indoors → more window-gazing

**Weighted average across all people with window access: ~3.5 minutes/day**

## Calculation

$$479{,}000{,}000 \text{ people} \times \frac{3.5 \text{ min}}{1440 \text{ min/day}} \times 365 \text{ days} \approx 424{,}000{,}000 \text{ person-days}$$

Rounding up slightly to account for occasional longer gazing bouts and micro-glances I may be underweighting:

## **My estimate: 450,000,000 person-days**

The dominant factor keeping this number relatively modest is that in 1800, the vast majority of humanity worked outdoors during all daylight hours, windows were small and often opaque, and artificial lighting made evening window-gazing pointless. The act of deliberately looking *through* a window was a small fraction of daily life for most people.
